title: Company Graph Relationship Rules
description: Defines allowed relationship types, valid source/target entity combinations, and semantic rules for edges in the Company Graph

relationship_types.description: An Actor is accountable for a Workflow or ValueObject. Implies primary responsibility.
relationship_types.example: Nadia Kovalchuk OWNS the Month-End Close workflow

relationship_types.description: An Actor made or co-made a Decision. Multiple actors can DECIDE on the same Decision.
relationship_types.example: Sarah Chen DECIDED the Salesforce over HubSpot selection

relationship_types.description: One entity has a direct causal impact on another. Used for Decision→Workflow, Decision→Commitment, Commitment→Actor, and ValueObject→Workflow.
relationship_types.example: The tech debt freeze (Decision) AFFECTS the SSO commitment (Commitment)

relationship_types.description: An Actor made a Commitment. The Actor is the maker of record; the recipient is captured in the commitment's made_to field.
relationship_types.example: Ravi Patel COMMITTED_TO the SSO delivery for Hartfield

relationship_types.description: A Workflow or ValueObject creates or generates another entity as its output. Captures data flow and dependency chains.
relationship_types.example: The Sales Handoff workflow PRODUCES the context fed into Onboarding

relationship_types.description: An Actor participates in or interacts with a Workflow without owning it. Lighter-weight than OWNS.
relationship_types.example: James Okafor TOUCHES the Customer Onboarding workflow when SSO setup is needed

relationship_types.description: Organizational reporting relationship between two Actors.
One entity prevents or delays another from proceeding. Strong causal dependency.
relationship_types.example: The tech debt freeze BLOCKS the Hartfield SSO commitment delivery

relationship_types.description: One entity requires another to be complete or healthy before it can function correctly.
relationship_types.example: The QBR Prep workflow DEPENDS_ON the Customer Data Platform

relationship_types.description: A Decision overrides or replaces a prior Decision. Captures decision evolution over time.
relationship_types.example: A new CRM decision would SUPERSEDE dec-001

relationship_types.description: A ValueObject or asset was created by a specific Actor or Workflow.
relationship_types.example: The Customer Data Platform was CREATED_BY Dev Anand

relationship_types.description: An Actor was a significant stakeholder in a Decision (but not a decision-maker). Captures input without responsibility.
relationship_types.example: Marcus Webb PARTICIPATED_IN the COO hiring decision without being the decider

validation_rules.description: from_id and to_id must be different values
All from_id and to_id values must reference entities that exist in the graph files
No two relationships can share the same id
Commitment.made_to should reference an actor-NNN or vo-NNN that exists in the graph

semantic_notes.ownership: Every Workflow should have at least one OWNS relationship from an Actor. Ownerless workflows are an institutional memory risk.
semantic_notes.decisions: Decisions without dissenting_views are incomplete — the absence of dissent is itself data and should be noted explicitly.
semantic_notes.commitments: Commitments with status 'overdue' or 'broken' that are not connected to any Decision represent untracked risk.
semantic_notes.value_objects: ValueObjects with health_status 'at_risk' or 'degraded' should have at least one Commitment or Decision connected to them describing the remediation plan.
